title: Calendar in Your Head
description: Learn the Doomsday Algorithm to calculate the day of the week for any date in history — entirely in your head. Master century codes, year codes, and month codes, then combine them into a lightning-fast mental calculation that will impress everyone you know.

modules.title: Foundation
modules.description: Understand the building blocks of the Doomsday Algorithm: day-number mapping, modular arithmetic, and century anchor codes.

lessons.title: Days and Numbers
lessons.description: Map each day of the week to a number and learn the modular arithmetic that powers the entire algorithm.

screens.title: What Day Were You Born?
screens.content:
Quick — **what day of the week were you born on?** Most people have no idea.

By the end of this course, you'll be able to answer that question in seconds — for *any* date in history. What day did the Titanic sink? What day will New Year's Eve fall on? You'll know instantly.

The secret is called the **Doomsday Algorithm**, a simple formula that breaks any date into small, easy pieces. No memorizing calendars. No counting on your fingers. Just a handful of codes and one short formula.

Let's start with the very first building block: turning days into numbers.
screens.callout: By the end of this course, you'll be the person at every party who can instantly name the day for any date!

screens.title: Days as Numbers
screens.content:
Every day of the week maps to a number from 0 to 6:

| Day | Number |
|---|---|
| Sunday | 0 |
| Monday | 1 |
| Tuesday | 2 |
| Wednesday | 3 |
| Thursday | 4 |
| Friday | 5 |
| Saturday | 6 |

Why these numbers? Because our formula uses **modular arithmetic** — specifically **mod 7**. When we divide any number by 7 and take the remainder, we always get 0 through 6, which maps perfectly to a day of the week.

For example, if our formula produces 11, we calculate: 11 mod 7 = **4** = **Thursday**.

If it produces 28, then 28 mod 7 = **0** = **Sunday**.

screens.title: Which number represents Wednesday?
screens.explanation: Wednesday is day 3 in our numbering system. Counting from Sunday (0): Sun=0, Mon=1, Tue=2, Wed=3. Think of it as Wednesday being three days after Sunday.

screens.title: What is 23 mod 7?
screens.explanation: 23 mod 7 = 2. Since 7 × 3 = 21, the remainder is 23 − 21 = 2. Modular arithmetic is the foundation of the Doomsday Algorithm — every calculation ends with a mod 7 step.
hints: What is the largest multiple of 7 that fits in 23?

screens.title: Fill In the Day Numbers
screens.prompt: In our numbering system, Monday is {{blank}} and Friday is {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: Monday is 1 (one day after Sunday) and Friday is 5 (five days after Sunday). The full mapping: Sun=0, Mon=1, Tue=2, Wed=3, Thu=4, Fri=5, Sat=6.
hints: Monday is the first day after Sunday

lessons.title: Century Codes
lessons.description: Memorize the four century anchor codes that form the first piece of the Doomsday Algorithm formula.

screens.title: What Are Century Codes?
screens.content:
The Doomsday Algorithm breaks date calculation into small, manageable pieces. The first piece is the **century code**.

Every century (100-year block) has a fixed code number. You only need to memorize **four values**, because they repeat in a cycle every 400 years.

Think of the century code as the "starting point" for any date in that century. It accounts for the accumulated drift of calendar days across hundreds of years.

The four century codes are the foundation — once you know them, you're already partway to calculating any date.

screens.title: The Four Century Codes
screens.content:
Here are the four century codes:

| Century | Code | Memory Aid |
|---|---|---|
| 1700s | 4 | "Seven-**four**-teen hundred" |
| 1800s | 2 | "Eighteen hundred = **2**" |
| 1900s | 0 | "Nineteen hundred = **zero**, fresh start" |
| 2000s | 6 | "Two thousand = **six**, like the letters in 'twenty'" |

The cycle repeats every 400 years: **4, 2, 0, 6**

So the 2100s use code 4 (same as 1700s), the 2200s use code 2 (same as 1800s), and so on.

**Most dates you'll encounter are in the 1900s (code 0) or 2000s (code 6).** Start by memorizing just these two!
screens.callout: Pro tip: 1900s = 0 is the easiest to remember, and it covers most dates in living memory!

screens.title: What is the century code for dates in the 1900s?
screens.explanation: The century code for the 1900s is 0. This is the easiest one to remember — zero for the nineteen-hundreds. It also makes calculations for 20th century dates simpler since adding 0 doesn't change the sum.

screens.title: Recall the Century Codes
screens.prompt: The century code for the 1700s is {{blank}} and for the 2000s is {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: The 1700s have century code 4 and the 2000s have century code 6. The complete cycle is: 1700s=4, 1800s=2, 1900s=0, 2000s=6, then it repeats.
hints: The 1700s code is the same as the number of letters in 'four'

screens.title: Using the repeating cycle, what is the century code for the 2100s?
screens.explanation: The century codes cycle every 400 years: 4, 2, 0, 6. Since 2100 is 400 years after 1700, it uses the same code: 4. The pattern: 1700s/2100s=4, 1800s/2200s=2, 1900s/2300s=0, 2000s/2400s=6.
hints: The codes repeat in a cycle of four

modules.title: The Algorithm
modules.description: Learn each component of the Doomsday formula step by step: year codes, month codes, and how to combine them all.

lessons.title: Year Codes Made Easy
lessons.description: Learn the formula to calculate the year code from the last two digits of any year.

screens.title: The Year Code Formula
screens.content:
The century code handles the first two digits of the year. Now we need a code for the **last two digits**.

The year code formula is:

**yearCode = (lastTwo + floor(lastTwo ÷ 4)) mod 7**

Where `lastTwo` is the last two digits of the year.

For example, for the year 1984:
- Last two digits: **84**
- floor(84 ÷ 4) = **21**
- (84 + 21) mod 7 = 105 mod 7 = **0**

So the year code for any year ending in '84 is **0**.

The `floor` function means we round down — we drop any remainder from the division by 4.

screens.title: Step-by-Step Walkthrough
screens.content:
Let's work through another example. For the year 2025:

1. Last two digits: **25**
2. Divide by 4 and round down: floor(25 ÷ 4) = **6**
3. Add to the original: 25 + 6 = **31**
4. Take mod 7: 31 mod 7 = **3**

So the year code for '25 is **3**.

**Mental math shortcut:** To find floor(N ÷ 4), just think "how many times does 4 go into N?" For 25, that's 6 (since 4 × 6 = 24). For 84, that's 21 (since 4 × 21 = 84).

With practice, you'll start to compute these instantly. The key is breaking it into two simple steps: divide by 4, then add and mod.

screens.title: Simplest Year Code
screens.prompt: For a year ending in 00: (0 + floor(0 ÷ 4)) mod 7 = {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: Any year ending in 00 has a year code of 0, because (0 + 0) mod 7 = 0. This makes years like 1900 and 2000 especially easy to work with!
hints: And zero mod 7 is still zero

screens.title: Calculate the Year Code for '84
screens.prompt: Year code for '84: floor(84 ÷ 4) = {{blank}}, so (84 + {{blank}}) mod 7 = {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: floor(84 ÷ 4) = 21 because 4 × 21 = 84 exactly. Then (84 + 21) = 105, and 105 mod 7 = 0 (since 7 × 15 = 105). The year code for '84 is 0.
hints: How many times does 4 go into 84?

screens.title: What is the year code for '69?
screens.explanation: floor(69 ÷ 4) = 17 (since 4 × 17 = 68). Then (69 + 17) = 86, and 86 mod 7 = 2 (since 7 × 12 = 84, and 86 − 84 = 2). The year code for '69 is 2.

screens.title: Year Code for '25
screens.explanation: floor(25 ÷ 4) = 6 (since 4 × 6 = 24). Then (25 + 6) = 31, and 31 mod 7 = 3 (since 7 × 4 = 28, and 31 − 28 = 3). The year code for '25 is 3.

lessons.title: Month Codes
lessons.description: Memorize the month codes using mnemonics and learn how leap years affect January and February.

screens.title: Month Codes Overview
screens.content:
Each month has a fixed code number that you'll need to memorize. The good news: there are patterns that make it easier.

Month codes tell us how many "extra days" each month contributes to the day-of-week calculation. They account for the fact that months have different lengths (28, 30, or 31 days).

There are 12 codes to learn, but several months share the same code, and there are tricks to remember them. Let's look at the full table.

screens.title: The Month Code Table
screens.content:
Here are the month codes for a **non-leap year**:

| Month | Code | Memory Aid |
|---|---|---|
| January | 0 | "**J**anuary starts at **z**ero" |
| February | 3 | "**Feb** has **3** letters before the vowel" |
| March | 3 | "**March** matches **Feb** (both 3)" |
| April | 6 | "**A**pril = **6** (A is the 1st letter, flip the 1 upside down = 6)" |
| May | 1 | "**May** Day = day **1**" |
| June | 4 | "**June** has **4** letters" |
| July | 6 | "**July** 4th → code **6** (think fireworks: 4+2=6)" |
| August | 2 | "**Aug**ust = **2** (A=1, u=2)" |
| September | 5 | "**S**eptember → **5** (S looks like 5)" |
| October | 0 | "**O**ctober = **0** (O looks like 0)" |
| November | 3 | "**Nov**ember = **3** (Nov has 3 letters)" |
| December | 5 | "**Dec**ember = **5** (D=4, +1=5)" |

**Key pattern:** January and October share code 0. February, March, and November share code 3. April and July share code 6. September and December share code 5.
screens.callout: January and October start on the same day of the week in non-leap years — that's because they both have code 0!

screens.title: What is the month code for July?
screens.explanation: July has a month code of 6. It shares this code with April. Remember: July 4th celebrations → think of fireworks and the number 6.
hints: July shares its code with April

screens.title: Recall the Month Codes
screens.prompt: The month code for March is {{blank}} and for September is {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: March has code 3 (same as February and November). September has code 5 (same as December). Remember: S looks like 5 for September.
hints: March shares its code with February
What does the letter S look like as a number?

screens.title: In a leap year, what is the month code for January?
screens.explanation: In a leap year, January's code changes from 0 to 6 (subtract 1, which wraps around: 0 − 1 = −1 ≡ 6 mod 7). February changes from 3 to 2. Only January and February are affected — all other months keep their normal codes.
hints: Leap years subtract 1 from January and February codes
Normal January = 0. Subtract 1 and think mod 7

screens.title: Leap Year February
screens.prompt: In a leap year, the month code for February changes from 3 to {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: In a leap year, February's code drops by 1: from 3 to 2. The leap day (February 29) shifts all dates in January and February back by one day compared to a non-leap year.
hints: Subtract 1 from the normal February code

lessons.title: Putting It All Together
lessons.description: Combine century codes, year codes, month codes, and the day into the complete Doomsday Algorithm formula.

screens.title: The Complete Formula
screens.content:
You now know all four ingredients. Here's the complete formula:

**dayOfWeek = (centuryCode + yearCode + monthCode + day) mod 7**

Where:
- **centuryCode** — from the century table (4, 2, 0, or 6)
- **yearCode** — calculated as (lastTwo + floor(lastTwo ÷ 4)) mod 7
- **monthCode** — from the month table (adjusted for leap years in Jan/Feb)
- **day** — the day of the month (1–31)

The result maps to a day: 0=Sun, 1=Mon, 2=Tue, 3=Wed, 4=Thu, 5=Fri, 6=Sat.

Let's practice combining all the pieces!
screens.callout: The complete formula: day = (centuryCode + yearCode + monthCode + day) mod 7

screens.title: Worked Example: July 4, 1776
screens.content:
Let's calculate what day of the week July 4, 1776 fell on — the signing of the Declaration of Independence.

**Step 1 — Century code:** 1700s → **4**

**Step 2 — Year code:** Last two digits = 76. floor(76 ÷ 4) = 19. (76 + 19) mod 7 = 95 mod 7 = **4**

**Step 3 — Month code:** July → **6**. (1776 is a leap year, but that only affects January and February, so no adjustment needed.)

**Step 4 — Day:** **4**

**Step 5 — Combine:** (4 + 4 + 6 + 4) mod 7 = 18 mod 7 = **4**

**Step 6 — Look up:** 4 = **Thursday**

The Declaration of Independence was signed on a **Thursday**!

screens.title: Put the Algorithm Steps in Order

items.text: Calculate the year code from the last two digits
Look up the month code (adjust for leap year if Jan/Feb)
Add all codes together with the day of the month
Convert the result (0–6) to a day name

screens.explanation: The algorithm follows a logical flow: first gather all the codes (century, year, month), then combine them with the day, reduce mod 7, and finally convert to a day name. The order of gathering codes can vary, but you must add before taking mod 7.
hints: Start by finding all the individual codes before combining them
You need to add everything before you can take mod 7

screens.title: Verify: July 4, 1776
screens.prompt: July 4, 1776: (4 + 4 + 6 + 4) mod 7 = {{blank}}, so it was a {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: 18 mod 7 = 4, and day number 4 = Thursday. The Declaration of Independence was indeed signed on a Thursday, July 4, 1776.

screens.title: Calculate: September 11, 2001
screens.prompt: September 11, 2001: Century(2000s) = 6, Year('01) = 1, Month(Sep) = 5, Day = 11. The sum mod 7 = {{blank}}, so it was a {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: (6 + 1 + 5 + 11) = 23, and 23 mod 7 = 2 = Tuesday. September 11, 2001 was indeed a Tuesday — a day the world will never forget.
hints: The sum is 23. Now find 23 mod 7

screens.title: Calculate: The Moon Landing
screens.prompt: July 20, 1969: Century(1900s) = 0, Year('69) = 2, Month(Jul) = 6, Day = 20. It was a {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: (0 + 2 + 6 + 20) = 28, and 28 mod 7 = 0 = Sunday. Neil Armstrong took humanity's first steps on the Moon on a Sunday, July 20, 1969.
hints: The sum is 28. What is 28 mod 7?
28 is exactly divisible by 7, so the remainder is 0 = ?

screens.title: You Know the Algorithm!
screens.content:
Congratulations — you now know the complete Doomsday Algorithm!

Let's recap the formula:

**dayOfWeek = (centuryCode + yearCode + monthCode + day) mod 7**

The four pieces:
1. **Century code:** 1700s=4, 1800s=2, 1900s=0, 2000s=6
2. **Year code:** (lastTwo + floor(lastTwo ÷ 4)) mod 7
3. **Month code:** Jan=0, Feb=3, Mar=3, Apr=6, May=1, Jun=4, Jul=6, Aug=2, Sep=5, Oct=0, Nov=3, Dec=5
4. **Leap year adjustment:** In leap years, Jan=6 and Feb=2 (subtract 1)

Result: Sun=0, Mon=1, Tue=2, Wed=3, Thu=4, Fri=5, Sat=6

You've already calculated three famous dates correctly. In the next module, we'll build speed and confidence with rapid-fire practice.

modules.title: Mastery
modules.description: Build speed and fluency with timed drills, write the algorithm in code, and learn how to use your new skill as a party trick.

lessons.title: Speed Drills
lessons.description: Practice calculating days for real dates with increasing difficulty, then implement the algorithm in code.

screens.title: Building Speed
screens.content:
You know the algorithm — now it's time to get **fast**.

Here are some mental math shortcuts:

- **1900s dates are easiest** because the century code is 0 — one less number to add.
- **Round years** (ending in 00, 04, 08, etc.) have simpler year codes because the division by 4 is clean.
- **Reduce as you go:** Don't wait until the end to mod 7. If your running sum exceeds 7, subtract 7 immediately.
- **Learn common year codes:** Years ending in 00→0, 01→1, 02→2, 03→3. The first few are just the number itself!

Let's practice with progressively harder dates. Try to work through each one mentally before filling in the answer.

screens.title: Easy: January 1, 2000
screens.prompt: January 1, 2000 is a leap year. Century = 6, Year = 0, Month(Jan, leap) = 6, Day = 1. It was a {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: (6 + 0 + 6 + 1) = 13, and 13 mod 7 = 6 = Saturday. The new millennium began on a Saturday! Note that 2000 is a leap year (divisible by 400), so January uses code 6 instead of 0.

screens.title: Medium: December 25, 2025
screens.prompt: What day of the week is Christmas 2025 (December 25, 2025)? {{blank}}
screens.explanation: Century(2000s) = 6, Year('25) = 3, Month(Dec) = 5, Day = 25. Sum = 6 + 3 + 5 + 25 = 39. 39 mod 7 = 4 = Thursday. Christmas 2025 falls on a Thursday!

screens.title: Hard: Einstein's Birthday
screens.prompt: Albert Einstein was born on March 14, 1879. What day was it? {{blank}}
screens.explanation: Century(1800s) = 2, Year('79) = (79 + 19) mod 7 = 98 mod 7 = 0, Month(Mar) = 3, Day = 14. Sum = 2 + 0 + 3 + 14 = 19. 19 mod 7 = 5 = Friday. Einstein was born on a Friday!
hints: Year code: floor(79 ÷ 4) = 19, so (79 + 19) mod 7 = 98 mod 7 = 0

screens.title: Hard: JFK Assassination
screens.prompt: President Kennedy was assassinated on November 22, 1963. What day was it? {{blank}}
screens.explanation: Century(1900s) = 0, Year('63) = (63 + 15) mod 7 = 78 mod 7 = 1, Month(Nov) = 3, Day = 22. Sum = 0 + 1 + 3 + 22 = 26. 26 mod 7 = 5 = Friday. JFK was assassinated on a Friday in Dallas, Texas.
hints: Year code: floor(63 ÷ 4) = 15, so (63 + 15) mod 7 = 78 mod 7 = 1

screens.title: Build the Day Calculator
screens.explanation: The algorithm combines century code, year code ((lastTwo + floor(lastTwo/4)) % 7), month code (with leap year adjustment: subtract 1 for Jan/Feb in leap years), and the day. Sum them all and take mod 7 to get the day index into the dayNames array.
hints: Use Math.floor(year / 100) to get the century number for the lookup
A year is a leap year if divisible by 4, except centuries unless divisible by 400
The formula is: (centuryCode + yearCode + monthCode + day) % 7

screens.title: Speed Tips Recap
screens.content:
Great work on those drills! Here are the speed tips to remember:

1. **Reduce early and often** — subtract 7 from your running total whenever it gets above 7.
2. **Know your common year codes** — years ending in 00, 01, 02, 03 have year codes of 0, 1, 2, 3.
3. **1900s are free** — the century code is 0, so you can skip it entirely.
4. **Pairs of months** share codes: Jan/Oct=0, Feb/Mar/Nov=3, Apr/Jul=6, Sep/Dec=5.
5. **Practice on dates you know** — birthdays, holidays, anniversaries.

With practice, you'll get each calculation down to under 10 seconds. Some mental math enthusiasts can do it in under 3!

lessons.title: Party Tricks
lessons.description: Apply your calendar skills to fun scenarios — historical events, future dates, and impressing friends.

screens.title: Your New Superpower
screens.content:
You've mastered the Doomsday Algorithm. Now let's put it to use in the real world!

**How to perform the trick:**
1. Ask someone to name **any date** — a birthday, anniversary, or random date from history.
2. While they're still talking, start calculating in your head.
3. Confidently announce the day of the week.
4. Watch their jaw drop as they verify on their phone.

**Tips for showmanship:**
- Pretend to think for a moment even if you know instantly — it makes it more impressive.
- Start with recent dates (2000s) since the century code 6 is easy to work with.
- If someone gives a date in the 1900s, celebrate internally — the century code is 0!

Let's practice with some fun historical dates.

screens.title: The Titanic sank on April 15, 1912. What day of the week was it?
screens.explanation: Century(1900s) = 0, Year('12) = (12 + 3) mod 7 = 15 mod 7 = 1, Month(Apr) = 6, Day = 15. Sum = 0 + 1 + 6 + 15 = 22. 22 mod 7 = 1 = Monday. The Titanic sank in the early hours of Monday, April 15, 1912.
hints: Century code for 1900s is the easiest to remember
Year code for '12: floor(12 ÷ 4) = 3, so (12 + 3) mod 7 = 1

screens.title: New Year's Eve 2025
screens.prompt: New Year's Eve 2025 (December 31, 2025) falls on a {{blank}}.
screens.explanation: Century(2000s) = 6, Year('25) = 3, Month(Dec) = 5, Day = 31. Sum = 6 + 3 + 5 + 31 = 45. 45 mod 7 = 3 = Wednesday. New Year's Eve 2025 is a Wednesday!
hints: You already know the year code for '25 — it's 3

screens.title: Halloween 2025
screens.explanation: Century(2000s) = 6, Year('25) = 3, Month(Oct) = 0, Day = 31. Sum = 6 + 3 + 0 + 31 = 40. 40 mod 7 = 5 = Friday. Halloween 2025 falls on a Friday — perfect for a spooky night out!
hints: October's month code is 0 — one of the easy ones

screens.title: Order the Party Trick Steps

items.text: Ask someone to name any date
Calculate the year code in your head
Recall the month code from memory
Add all codes plus the day, reduce mod 7
Announce the day of the week confidently

screens.explanation: First get the date from your audience. Then mentally work through the algorithm: century code, year code, month code. Sum everything with the day, take mod 7, and announce the result. The order of computing the three codes can vary, but you must gather all pieces before summing.
hints: You need a date before you can calculate anything
Gather all the codes, then combine them

screens.title: You Did It!
screens.content:
**Congratulations — you've mastered the Doomsday Algorithm!**

You can now calculate the day of the week for any date in history (or the future) using nothing but mental math. Here's what you've learned:

- **Days as numbers:** Sun=0 through Sat=6
- **Century codes:** 4, 2, 0, 6 (repeating every 400 years)
- **Year codes:** (lastTwo + floor(lastTwo ÷ 4)) mod 7
- **Month codes:** 12 values with mnemonic tricks
- **Leap year rule:** Subtract 1 from January and February codes
- **The formula:** (centuryCode + yearCode + monthCode + day) mod 7

**What to do next:**
- Practice with birthdays of friends and family
- Calculate historical dates and verify with a search engine
- Challenge people to stump you — they won't be able to!
- Try to get your calculation time under 5 seconds

You now possess a genuine mathematical superpower. Go share it with the world!
screens.callout: You now have a genuine mathematical superpower. Go impress someone with it today!
